content: Leonardo designed several flying machines including the ornithopter (flapping-wing aircraft), an aerial screw (helicopter precursor), and a hang glider. He studied bird flight obsessively, filling notebooks with detailed observations of wing mechanics. When engineers tested his glider design 500 years later, it actually flew. He was simply born centuries too early for powered flight.

The Mona Lisa, painted between 1503-1519, is the most famous painting in the world. Leonardo used his revolutionary sfumato technique — subtle blending of colors and tones without visible brushstrokes. The painting's mysterious smile changes depending on the viewer's angle, a deliberate optical illusion. It now hangs in the Louvre Museum in Paris behind bulletproof glass.

The Last Supper, painted between 1495-1498 on a wall of the Santa Maria delle Grazie monastery in Milan, depicts the moment Jesus announces one of his disciples will betray him. Leonardo captured each apostle's individual emotional reaction. The painting began deteriorating within years due to Leonardo's experimental fresco technique.

Leonardo dissected over 30 human corpses and created detailed anatomical drawings centuries ahead of their time. His drawing of the Vitruvian Man demonstrates the proportions of the human body. He accurately depicted the heart's chambers, the fetus in the womb, and the vascular system. His anatomical work was not published in his lifetime and was only rediscovered centuries later.

Leonardo excelled in painting, sculpture, architecture, science, mathematics, engineering, anatomy, geology, astronomy, botany, and music. He embodied the Renaissance ideal of the 'universal man' — someone who develops all their talents fully. He kept detailed notebooks written in mirror script (right to left), possibly for secrecy or simply because he was left-handed.

Leonardo designed bridges, canals, armored vehicles (tank precursors), diving suits, and machine guns centuries before they were built. He designed a self-propelled cart (precursor to the automobile) powered by springs. His engineering notebooks contain designs for ball bearings, concentrated solar power, and a calculator.

Leonardo believed art and science were inseparable — both stemming from observation and curiosity. He said: 'Study the science of art. Study the art of science. Develop your senses — especially learn to see. Realize that everything connects to everything else.' His paintings demonstrate deep understanding of optics, geometry, and human anatomy.

Leonardo was born on April 15, 1452, in Vinci, near Florence, Italy. He was the illegitimate son of a notary and a peasant woman. He apprenticed in the workshop of Andrea del Verrocchio in Florence, where he learned painting, sculpture, and engineering. According to legend, when Leonardo painted an angel in one of Verrocchio's works that surpassed the master's own figures, Verrocchio put down his brush forever.
